coredata.dc:title: Ultrafast magneto-inductive synthesis of carbon dots from plant-based precursors using deep eutectic solvents: A comparative study with traditional hydrothermal methods

coredata.dc:description: The batch hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) has limitations in terms of yields and production capacity. This work aimed to develop an efficient and rapid technique to synthesize CDs through magneto-induction from natural plant-based precursors. Firstly, the influence of temperature and duration in a batch hydrothermal method was studied, using lignin, palm empty fruit bunch (EFB), glucose, sucrose, and xylose as the precursors. Green and sustainable deep eutectic solvents (DESs), specifically choline chloride (ChCl)/lactic acid (ChLa), ChCl/urea (ChUr), ChCl/1,3 butanediol (ChBu), and ChCl/oxalic acid (ChOx), were utilized in the batch hydrothermal synthesis of CDs. This study demonstrated that the CDs derived from ChLa and ChOx showed a high quantum yield (%QY) when lignin was the precursor. In contrast, the CDs from ChLa and ChBu demonstrated a high %QY for biomass EFB precursor, and the CDs from ChUr and ChBu achieved a high %QY for glucose, sucrose, and xylose precursors, respectively. ChBu demonstrated a well-balanced %QY and promising CD characteristics alongside a notably higher mass yield across all precursors, making it the ideal choice for the magneto-inductive synthesis study. Magnetic hyperthermia (MHT) stands out by producing superior CDs with an impressive QY of 23.44 % and a remarkable mass yield of 28.85 %. The MHT method generated the highest CD productivity of 62.72 mg CDs/g Lig/h from lignin in ChBu solvent at 25 wt% Fe3O4 in only 180 sec (ChBuLig25wtFe180s). This was 11.18 times higher than the conventional hydrothermal method, which required an exogenous heat source at 220 °C for 10 h. The MHT method cut production costs by 13.41 times compared to traditional hydrothermal methods. This significant cost-saving stems primarily from its ability to dramatically shorten processing times, enhance heat transfer efficiency to the carbon nanostructures, and reduce overall expenses. These advantages culminate in a more efficient, streamlined, and practical production process, delivering superior CD productivity.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The batch hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) has limitations in terms of yields and production capacity. This work aimed to develop an efficient and rapid technique to synthesize CDs through magneto-induction from natural plant-based precursors. Firstly, the influence of temperature and duration in a batch hydrothermal method was studied, using lignin, palm empty fruit bunch (EFB), glucose, sucrose, and xylose as the precursors. Green and sustainable deep eutectic solvents (DESs), specifically choline chloride (ChCl)/lactic acid (ChLa), ChCl/urea (ChUr), ChCl/1,3 butanediol (ChBu), and ChCl/oxalic acid (ChOx), were utilized in the batch hydrothermal synthesis of CDs. This study demonstrated that the CDs derived from ChLa and ChOx showed a high quantum yield (%QY) when lignin was the precursor. In contrast, the CDs from ChLa and ChBu demonstrated a high %QY for biomass EFB precursor, and the CDs from ChUr and ChBu achieved a high %QY for glucose, sucrose, and xylose precursors, respectively. ChBu demonstrated a well-balanced %QY and promising CD characteristics alongside a notably higher mass yield across all precursors, making it the ideal choice for the magneto-inductive synthesis study. Magnetic hyperthermia (MHT) stands out by producing superior CDs with an impressive QY of 23.44 % and a remarkable mass yield of 28.85 %. The MHT method generated the highest CD productivity of 62.72 mg CDs/g Lig/h from lignin in ChBu solvent at 25 wt% Fe3O4 in only 180 sec (ChBuLig25wtFe180s). This was 11.18 times higher than the conventional hydrothermal method, which required an exogenous heat source at 220 °C for 10 h. The MHT method cut production costs by 13.41 times compared to traditional hydrothermal methods. This significant cost-saving stems primarily from its ability to dramatically shorten processing times, enhance heat transfer efficiency to the carbon nanostructures, and reduce overall expenses. These advantages culminate in a more efficient, streamlined, and practical production process, delivering superior CD productivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots, or CDs, are nanomaterials made of carbon that are widely produced and used, especially in biological imaging and as sensors to measure biological substances, because they have great luminescent properties, can give off a wide range of colors, and are very stable in light (Baker and Baker, 2010). Furthermore, CDs have the advantages of being compatible with living cells, having low toxicity, being water-soluble, and easily being modified for the functional groups on their surface specifically towards target deliverable molecules at a low manufacturing cost (Li et al., 2012). CDs are additionally known for having unique optical, electronic, and chemical properties. They have become useful building blocks for many types of applications, such as drug nanocarriers (Sangjan et al., 2022), bioimaging, medical detection (Saengsrichan et al., 2022a), environmental sensing (Saengsrichan et al., 2022b), and photo-responsive nanomaterials such as light harvesting and photosensitizers or photocatalytic reactive oxygen species inducers (Sakdaronnarong et al., 2020). The quest for innovative, efficient, and sustainable approaches to address the growing demand for advanced nanomaterials has significantly propelled the development of CD synthesis methods. However, conventional synthesis techniques are often hindered by lengthy reaction times, harsh conditions, and limited control over critical parameters, posing challenges to scalability and reproducibility.

Researchers have developed several technologies for the large-scale production of CDs, including scalable hydrothermal processes in continuous-flow reactors (Baragau et al., 2021; Campalani and Rigo, 2023; Shao et al., 2019; Supajaruwong et al., 2023), microwave-assisted synthesis (de Medeiros et al., 2019; Shibata et al., 2022), and scalable hydrothermal methods using high boiling point solvents such as glycerol (Mai et al., 2020) and polyethylene glycol (Sangjan et al., 2022). These approaches not only ensure high production yields but also maintain the desired optical and chemical properties of CDs required for various applications. When it comes to increasing the production capacity of CDs to meet industrial needs, combining continuous flow (CF) technologies with bottom-up methods, especially hydrothermal or solvothermal routes, is one of the fastest ways when full control over the CD's size and properties could be accomplished. CF technology enables the management of controlling the critical synthetic variables, such as heat and mass transfer, which are difficulties in conventional methods. The ability to tightly regulate reaction parameters such as temperature, flow rates, and pressure allows the production of CDs with more consistent and uniform characteristics. However, the dimensions of flow reactors generally range from sub-millimetric (microfluidic reactors) (Shao et al., 2019) to sub-centimetric (meso-fluidic reactors) (Gérardy et al., 2018; Qiao et al., 2013). These configurations enhance reaction by easily pressurizing and heating reagents above their normal boiling point, a process known as superheating. However, our understanding and experience of the CF process for CD production highlights the difficulties in handling solids, the necessity to redefine product traceability (Gérardy et al., 2018), and the constraints of utilizing low precursor concentrations to avoid polymerization (Supajaruwong et al., 2023). In the CF process for CD synthesis, a high concentration of precursors exceeding 5 %w/v in the aqueous phase can significantly trigger the polymerization of an organic compound, leading to fouling and clogging issues within the pressurized system. Addressing these challenges requires process development and intensification, focusing on optimizing the reaction recipe and refining reactor configurations. It has been reported that solvents have been found to play an important role in dissolving polymeric precursors, adding heteroatoms to nanoparticles, and enhancing catalytic activity.

Deep eutectic solvents (DESs), consisting of hydrogen bond donors (HBD) and hydrogen bond acceptors (HBA), have recently emerged as sustainable alternatives for traditional solvents, owing to their low toxicity, biodegradability, and tunable characteristics. The unique physicochemical properties of DESs, along with their compatibility with diverse precursor materials, make them ideal reaction media for CD synthesis. By harnessing the synergistic effects of ultrafast magnetic-assisted techniques and DESs, researchers can achieve remarkable control over CD synthesis, paving the way for tailored nanomaterials with enhanced characteristics such as their tunable hydrophobicity and hydrophilicity (Çalhan et al., 2018). Choline chloride (ChCl) is commonly known as HBA. ChCl-based DESs have been found to enhance lignin dissolution and extraction from biomass and loosen cellulose structure (Nosri et al., 2024; Sonyeam et al., 2024). Several studies on DES systems have focused on utilizing ChCl–based DESs for extraction (Li et al., 2018), digestion of supramolecules (Panhwar et al., 2018), and synthesis of compounds (Jiang et al., 2018). The mechanistic research of ChCl–based DESs has been elucidated and could be categorized into several groups based on HBDs, including 1) basic DES such as ChCl–urea or ChCl–thiourea (Abbott et al., 2003; Tabaraki and Nazari, 2023; Valencia et al., 2023), 2) poly-alcoholic DES or diol DES such as ChCl–glycerol, ChCl–ethylene glycol (Nosri et al., 2024), and 3) acidic DES such as ChCl–carboxylic acid e.g., ChCl-oxalic acid (Tabaraki and Nazari, 2023), ChCl–citric acid (Tabaraki and Nazari, 2023), ChCl–lactic acid (Sonyeam et al., 2024). These ChCl–based DESs have emerged as significant solvents in synthetic chemistry, catalyst applications, separation processes, and electrochemistry. Apart from that, DESs are highly organized supramolecular solvents with considerable potential for the controlled synthesis of nanoparticles. DESs has been utilized as a custom solvent for the fabrication of various valuable nanoparticles, such as gold (Raghuwanshi et al., 2014), silver (Oseguera-Galindo et al., 2016), calcium phosphate (Karimi et al., 2016b), Cu2ZnSnS4 (CZTS) (Karimi et al., 2016a), magnesium, cobalt, and nickel ferrite (Söldner et al., 2016). Previous research reported successful CD synthesis from sugar and biomass using diethylene glycol (Liu et al., 2014) and polyethylene glycol (Ling et al., 2020; Sangjan et al., 2022) resulting in CDs with strong QY. The results revealed that the fluorescent properties of CDs are affected by reagents, solvents, passivation agents, the synthesis procedures, and conditions. However, to the best of our knowledge, no previous CD synthesis has been performed in DESs using a magnetic inductive molecular heating approach.

In this context, the integration of magnetic-assisted techniques, so-called magnetic hyperthermia (MHT), with the DES system stands as a cutting-edge approach poised to revolutionize the landscape of CD fabrication. Utilizing magnetic fields to expedite CD synthesis offers several compelling advantages, including enhanced reaction kinetics, accurate particle size, morphology control, and facile separation of CDs from reaction media. Furthermore, the interaction between magnetic fields and DESs accelerates the solvation process, promoting uniform dispersion of precursor materials and facilitating the production of high-quality CDs. Ultrafast magnetic-assisted synthesis method offers a promising solution to these challenges by enabling the rapid, precise, and scalable production of CDs with the desired properties. This study aimed to explore the synthesis of CDs from plant-based precursors, including palm bunch, lignin, xylose, glucose, and sucrose, using DESs as the reaction medium. The synthesis methods compared were the conventional batch hydrothermal method and the ultrafast magnetic hyperthermia approach. Additionally, the peroxidase-like activity of the synthesized CDs as nanozymes was investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material and methods
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: D(+)-xylose (99 +%), D(+)-glucose (≥99.5 %), and D(+)-sucrose (>99.5 %) were analytical grade and purchased from Daejung (Korea), Sigma-Aldrich (Germany), and Carlo Erba (France), respectively. Lignin, choline chloride (≥99 %), lactic acid (≥85 %), and 1,3- butanediol (≥99 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Urea (99.5 %), and oxalic acid (95–100 %) were supplied from Fisher Scientific (Belgium) and Daejung (Korea), respectively. Magnetic iron (II, III) oxide (Fe3O4) (≥99.6 %) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). All the chemicals were obtained from commercial sources, and used without the purification process. Palm empty fruit bunch (EFB) was obtained from a palm oil mill in Thailand. The solutions were prepared using ultrapure water purified by a water purification system (New Human UP System, Korea) with an electrical resistance of 18.2 MΩ-cm. Sulfuric acid (98 %, HPLC grade) was purchased from Merck (Germany). Quinine sulfate dihydrate (99 +%) was purchased from Acros Organics (Belgium).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ChCl-based DESs
ce:section.ce:para: ChCl which is an HBD molecule was mixed with various HBA molecules including lactic acid, urea, 1,3 butanediol, and oxalic acid. The optimal molar ratio of HBD:HBA from our previous research work was used (Nosri et al., 2024; Sonyeam et al., 2024) namely ChCl: lactic acid (1:10), ChCl:Urea (1:2), ChCl:1,3 Butanediol (1:2), and ChCl:oxalic acid (1:1). And the synthesized DESs were named as ChLa, ChUr, ChBu, and ChOx, respectively. The solid form of precursors was weighed, mixed, and transformed to liquid phase after stirring vigorously at 80 °C, 200 rpm for 60 min resulting in a clear liquid form at room temperature (25 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD synthesis by hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: Precursors (2 g) were mixed with 18 mL of DESs and 2 mL of ultrapure water type I. Then, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at various temperatures (200, 220, and 240 °C) for different periods (6 and 10 h). The reaction was naturally cooled to room temperature after completion. The synthesized CDs solution was centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 20 min at 20 °C followed by filtering through a 0.22 µm filter to remove large insoluble particles. The solution was further dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) for 48 h to remove all impurities such as furan derivatives, residual sugars, and organic acids (Li et al., 2017; Sangjan et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD synthesis by MHT method
ce:section.ce:para: Precursor (6.25 g) i.e., sucrose or lignin was mixed with 50 mL DES solution. Fe3O4 nanoparticles with various concentrations of 5, 15, and 25 %wt were added to a custom-made quartz column with a gas release mechanism, which was placed into an inductive copper coil (S.A.J.I. (Thailand), Co., Ltd.) of a magnetic hyperthermia reactor. The mixture was treated with an alternative electromagnetic field (220 V, 100 kHz, 13 kW) for 120 s and 180 s. After reaction completion, the mixture was cooled to room temperature naturally and deionized water was added to the column to dissolve the CDs. Subsequently, Fe3O4 nanoparticles were removed by magnetic field and the centrifugation at 5500 rpm for 15 min at 20 °C was carried out. The residual precursor was removed by centrifugation at 11,000 rpm for 20 min at 20 °C. Then, the CDs solution was purified by filtration through a 0.22 μm filter and dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) for 48 h. A schematic illustration of CDs synthesis using the MHT method is shown in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence intensity of CDs was measured using a spectrofluorometer (Model FP-6200, Perkin Elmer, Japan) with excitation wavelengths ranging from 240 to 400 nm, with a 20 nm increment. The UV-Vis absorbance over a range of 200–700 nm was measured using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Model UV-1800, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Japan). The energy bandgap was calculated using Tauc plot (Supajaruwong et al., 2023). The quantum yield (%QY) of CDs was calculated using Eq. (1) when the reference (ref) is quinine sulfate (QY=54 %) dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution, I is the peak area of maximum fluorescence (FL) intensity, A is the absorbance at the maximum excitation wavelength, and η is the refractive index of the solution (Wei et al., 2017).

The colloidal stability of CDs was determined by the surface charge using a Zeta potential analyzer (NanoPartica SZ-100V2 Series, Malvern Panalytical, Japan). The measurement was done in triplicates to find the average charge and standard deviation. The surface elemental composition of CDs was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Model PHI5000 VersaProbe II, ULVAC-PHI Inc., Japan) and the spectral fitting was performed using PHI MultiPak XPS software. Raman spectroscopy (XPloRA Plus, Horiba, France) and X-ray diffractometry (XRD) (Model D2 PHASER, BRUKER, Germany) were employed to determine the atomic and crystal structure of CDs, respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed using the Nicolet 6700, Thermo Scientific, USA, with attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode to evaluate the functional groups of CDs. A field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM) (Model JEM-3100F, JEOL, Japan) at the accelerating voltage of 300 kV was applied to analyze the CDs morphology and particle size distribution. The thermal stability of 15 mg of freeze-dried materials was assessed using a simultaneous thermal analyzer (NETZSCH, STA 449 F3 Jupiter, Selb, Germany). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) were performed by heating the samples from 30°C to 900°C at a rate of 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase mimetic activity study
ce:section.ce:para: To study the peroxidase-like activity of CDs, 10 mM hydrogen peroxide and 5 mM 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) solution were prepared as the stock solution. The assay started by pipetting 600 μL of TMB stock solution into a 5 mL microtube, and then 100 μL of the CDs solution was added, followed by an exact volume of sodium acetate buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 4.0) to obtain 3 mL total volume. The final step was to add 3.4 μL of the hydrogen peroxide solution (30 wt%) into the reaction mixture. After completing the reaction solution preparation, the reaction proceeded for 30 min at room temperature (25 °C). The measurement of the absorbance using UV-Vis spectrophotometry at a specific wavelength of 652 nm of oxidized TMB was carried out for kinetic study. To evaluate the peroxidase-like activity of CDs, the color intensity of oxidized TMB was compared with the standard curve of hydrogen peroxide solutions at concentrations ranging from 0.01 mM to 1 M H2O2 (Saengsrichan et al., 2022a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
ce:section.ce:para: Based on our previous research, particular ChCl-based DESs effectively dissolve unique type of precursors such as palm empty fruit bunch, lignin, and cellulose which are complex structures or hetero-/homo- polymers that require to be loosened from the matrix. Dissolved molecules by DESs are afterward cleaved into monomers for further condensation, dehydration, and carbonization to produce short-chain oligomers (Nosri et al., 2024), building block chemicals or nanoparticles (Jahir Khan et al., 2024; Sonyeam et al., 2024). As demonstrated in Table S1, distinct DESs could be functionally selected for biomass dissolution, lignin extraction, carbohydrate extraction, and catalytic cleavage. Table S1

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from lignin, EFB, and sugars in DESs
Quantum and mass yields of hydrothermal CDs from lignin, EFB, and sugars
ce:section.ce:para: Based on FL and UV-Vis absorbance properties, the %QYs of CDs synthesized through batch hydrothermal reactor at 200 °C for 6 h when the precursors were lignin, EFB, glucose, sucrose, and xylose in various types of DESs are shown in Fig. S1. The lignin precursor achieved high %QY when using ChOx (93.6 %QY) and ChLa (63.2 %QY) solvents, while EFB was suitable for ChLa (167.6 %QY) and ChBu (162.5 %QY) solvents. It was reported that acidic DESs assist in degrading the structure of lignin and lignocellulose in palm bunches (Vigier et al., 2015). In case of sugar precursors namely sucrose, glucose, and xylose, ChUr and ChBu were the most appropriate solvents to produce CDs with high %QY. This was possibly due to the fluorophore surface that markedly enhanced their FL intensities (Baunsgaard et al., 2000). The mass yields of the aforementioned CDs are demonstrated in Fig. S2. The diol-based DES which was ChBu gave the highest mass yield of CDs for all precursors which were 26.67 %, 0.44 %, 0.21 %, 0.96 %, and 0.47 % from lignin, EFB, glucose, sucrose, and xylose, respectively. Since the mass yield of CDs from ChBu was substantially greater than those of other DESs, therefore, ChBu solvent was selected for further CD synthesis due to high %QY and %mass yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic characterization of hydrothermal CDs from lignin, EFB, and sugars
ce:section.ce:para: The FL property of lignin-derived CDs is shown in Fig. S3. All the CDs synthesized from lignin using different DESs (ChLa, ChUr, ChBu, and ChOx) exhibited maximum emission wavelength (λEm) around 400 nm when the excitation wavelength (λEx) was 280 nm, except for CDs from ChUr which displayed the maximum emission at λEm = ∼400 nm when λEx was 240 nm. In case of EFB-derived CDs in different DESs as illustrated in Fig. S4, two prominent FL emission peaks at λEm = ∼400 and ∼500 nm were observed. This was possibly due to the fractionation of lignin and hemicellulose under hydrothermal reaction in DESs, which could generate CDs with distinct forms of carbon core structures surrounded by fluorophore molecules. EFB-derived CDs from ChLa and ChBu exhibited the peak excitation at λEx = 280 nm, resulting in maximum emission intensity. In comparison, EFB-CDs synthesized from ChUr and ChOx shifted the excitation wavelength from λEx = 280 nm to 340 nm and 380 nm, respectively. The latter was called red-shift phenomena, and thus the CDs from EFB in the presence of ChUr and ChOx DESs were prone to be able to apply in the visible light to near-infrared (NIR) irradiation for other applications such as solar photocatalysis and in vivo NIR photothermal therapy (Saengsrichan et al., 2022a; Supajaruwong et al., 2023; Wibowo et al., 2024).

CDs synthesized from all sugars including glucose, sucrose, and xylose in different DESs gave a promising fluorescence emission with excitation-independent characteristics as illustrated in Fig. S5-S7, respectively. It was found that CDs synthesized from sugars in DESs (ChLa, ChUr, ChBu, and ChOx) achieved maximum emission in a wide range of excitation wavelengths from 280 nm to 360 nm. This unique characteristic provided important information for further investigation on CD's application in light-harvesting ability from ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared (NIR) irradiation that can be applied in both a solar cell and biomedical applications as well as photocatalytic reactivity under natural sunlight. In conclusion, the FL spectroscopic analysis revealed that lignin and EFB-derived CDs exhibited FL emission in the peak wavelength range of λEm = 400–420 nm, while glucose, sucrose, and xylose showed emission in the peak wavelength range of λEm = 370–530 nm. Interestingly, ChBu could shift also the maximum excitation wavelength from λEx = 280 nm to 360 nm for sucrose-derived CD (Fig. S6C) and xylose-derived CD (Fig. S7C). These findings referred to the phenomenon at which the absorption spectrum was shifted towards a greater wavelength (red-shift) when precursors are sucrose and xylose for ChBu DESs. The red-shifted CDs have been reported to be promising nanomaterials for photothermal therapy and photocatalysis under a wider spectrum of natural sunlight e.g., water splitting, or solar cell application (Ma et al., 2019; Saini et al., 2022; Sakdaronnarong et al., 2020).

More insightful characterization on the photonic band gap measurement using UV-Vis absorbance and Tauc Plot of scanning wavelength from 200 to 700 nm for all CDs in different DESs was demonstrated in Fig. S8. The results showed that ChOx gave CDs with the narrowest band gap relative to other DESs when lignin, EFB, and glucose were precursors as illustrated in Fig. S8(A), S8(B), and S8(C), respectively. ChBu gave CDs with the narrowest band gap when sucrose and xylose were the precursors (Fig. S8(D) and S8(E), respectively). These findings corresponded to the red-shift phenomena aforementioned since the narrower bandgap could perfectly fit to longer-wavelength light excitation that was caused by 1) an increase in CDs size (quantum confinement effect) (Wang and Lu, 2022), or 2) added nitrogen heteroatoms or oxygen containing defects in carbon structure (Pongchaikul et al., 2023; Saengsrichan et al., 2022b). In all CD samples, UV-Vis absorbance of CDs synthesized in ChBu exhibited strongest peaks at 280 nm representing fluorophore surface passivation which could enhance the %QY and shift the λEx to longer wavelength from UV to NIR spectra.

Fig. S8(F) demonstrates the relationship between band gap energy and measured hydrodynamic diameter of CDs from dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique while Fig. S8(G) presents the correlation of TEM size of CDs from both hydrothermal and MHT synthesis versus the energy band gap. Measuring particle size in the water medium, indicating a high likelihood of CD agglomeration, which could potentially account for the CDs' large size. A negative linear relationship between band gap energy and both hydrodynamic diameter and TEM particle size of CDs showed high coefficients of determination (R2) of 0.94 and 0.80, respectively, while lower R2 of 0.54 was obtained from MHT derived CDs from which the model of MHT derived CDs is required to be further optimized. These results indicated that when the size of CDs is small, the energy value of the gap between the energy bands increases. On the other hand, when the size of CDs is large, it reduces the gap between the energy band which is in good agreement with the quantum confinement effect (Abdellatif et al., 2022). In brief, the size of CDs affects the band gap energy. This is consistent with the red-shift phenomenon that occurs in glucose, sucrose, and xylose because the synthesized sugars have larger particle sizes than lignin and EFB, thus a reduced band gap found in glucose, sucrose, and xylose caused red-shift emission from λEm = ∼400 nm to ∼500 nm which is in the range of NIR spectrum (Li et al., 2022).

Structural characterization of CDs synthesized from lignin (Lig), and EFB in ChLa and ChBu, as well as CDs from glucose (Glu), sucrose (Suc), and xylose (Xyl) in ChUr and ChBu in a hydrothermal reactor at 200 °C for 6 h, was performed by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S9). All CDs samples presented graphitic carbon structure (G band) at 1550–1590 cm−1 and defected or disordered carbon structure (D band) at 1350–1375 cm−1 in Raman spectra. Graphitic carbon in CDs was attributed to sp2 hybridization of the graphite-like structure, while sp and sp3 hybridizations were found either due to the defect/disordered structure of the graphitic carbon core or the amorphous structure of carbon (Pimenta et al., 2007). As shown in Fig. S9, different Raman intensities, and peak areas of the G and D bands were obtained from different CDs from different DESs. To explain the characteristics of CDs in detail, ID/IG ratio of all CDs was calculated as demonstrated in Table 1. The lower ID/IG values indicate that the carbon nanomaterials have an increased graphitic plane orientation more than the amorphous structure arrangement. Analysis results of ID/IG showed a greater fraction of amorphous carbon than graphitic carbon arrangement in CDs synthesized from lignin in ChBu (ChBuLig, ID/IG = 1.11) and EFB-CDs synthesized in ChBu (ChBuEFB, ID/IG = 1.07). The presumably highest graphitic carbon structure and less defect or disordered carbon core expressed by low ID/IG ratio was observed in ChLaEFB followed by ChUrSuc and ChUrXyl having ID/IG of 0.15, 0.58, and 0.62, respectively. A low ID/IG additionally represents a substantially less amount of both amorphous and graphitic carbon in CDs (Das et al., 2016; Qu et al., 2014). However, apart from ID/IG ratio, the IG and ID intensity and area representing carbon content in material are necessary to be considered.

The XRD results supported the Raman spectroscopy and ID/IG ratio aforementioned. As shown in Fig. S10, the XRD of CDs from lignin and EFB in ChLa, and ChBu, and CDs from glucose, sucrose, and xylose in ChUr and ChBu solvents showed broad peaks with 2θ range of 17° and 24° indicating the 002 peak formation of the crystallographic arrangement of carbon structure (Azam et al., 2021; Mintz et al., 2021). The strongest intensity of XRD peak at ∼20° was obtained from ChBuLig > ChLaLig > ChBuEFB for lignin and EFB precursor (Fig. S10A), and ChBuXyl > ChUrXyl > ChBuSuc for sugar precursors (Fig. S10B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic studies of hydrothermal CDs from lignin, EFB, and sugars
ce:section.ce:para: FETEM image analysis of CDs synthesized from lignin and EFB in ChLa and ChBu as well as CDs synthesized from glucose, sucrose, and xylose in ChUr and ChBu using hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 6 h was demonstrated in Fig. S11. From the FETEM results, the particle size of CDs synthesized from all DESs was in the nanometer range. CDs had spherical shape and were well dispersed in aqueous solution. Average particle sizes of CDs from lignin and EFB synthesized in ChLa, and ChBu solvents were 4.48 ± 1.26 (ChLaLig), 5.12 ± 1.72 (ChBuLig), 5.32 ± 1.75 (ChLaEFB), and 52.51 ± 12.70 nm (ChBuEFB). The average size of CDs synthesized from different sugars were 4.16 ± 1.07 (ChBuXyl), 5.36 ± 1.20 (ChUrXyl), 6.60 ± 1.41 (ChBuSuc), 87.80 ± 27.30 (ChBuGlu), 128.42 ± 31.41 (ChUrSuc), and 151.40 ± 27.30 nm (ChUrGlu). From the TEM results, ChUr seemingly produced CDs with less colloidal stability and thus aggregation could occur resulting in particle size of CDs greater than 100 nm. In contrast, ChBu yielded CDs with smaller sizes than 10 nm for both groups of precursors, particularly from lignin, xylose, and sucrose. In addition, ChBu even facilitates better particle size distribution, and superior CDs dispersion which could be supported by zeta potential values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface ζ potential of CDs synthesized by hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: The surface charge of CDs from different DESs using zeta potential measurement is demonstrated in Fig. S12. CDs synthesized from lignin and EFB in different DESs (ChLa, ChBu, ChUr, ChOx) and CDs from glucose, sucrose, and xylose in ChLa, ChBu, ChOx solvents exhibited negatively charged surfaces, whereas the CDs synthesized from glucose, sucrose, and xylose in ChUr solvent tend to increase the charge to a positive value which was possibly due to the presence of more amine groups on the surface of the CDs in ChUr solvent (Zhu et al., 2019). Therefore, lower zeta potential and less colloidal stability were obtained in case of ChUr. ChBu, in contrast, displayed stronger negatively charged CDs from lignin (ChBuLig), EFB (ChBuEFB), and sucrose (ChBuSuc). From the overall characterizations, we selected ChLa and ChBu solvents for lignin, while ChUr and ChBu were chosen for sucrose in the subsequent optimization of temperature and time of CD synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature and time on the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from lignin and sucrose in selected solvents
QY, mass yield, and surface ζ potential of hydrothermal CDs synthesized from lignin and sucrose
ce:section.ce:para: To select the suitable temperature and time for CDs synthesis in DESs, lignin precursor was selected from lignocellulosic groups (lignin and EFB) and ChLa and ChBu solvents were used. Among sugar precursors (glucose, sucrose, and xylose), sucrose was selected to synthesize CDs in ChUr and ChBu solvents via hydrothermal conditions. The selection was based on their characteristics of high %QY, FL emission, and mass yield, together with well-structured carbon, small average size, and good dispersibility in aqueous solution. A factorial design was applied for the experimental setup, varying the synthesis temperatures (200, 220, and 240 °C) and times (6 and 10 h).

It was found that the QY of all CDs samples synthesized from lignin and sucrose increased as the temperature and time increased ( Fig. 1). The results were in good agreement with the previous report due to more carbonization of organic precursors (Nxele and Nyokong, 2022). The synthesis temperature for the high QY was in the range of 220–240 °C compared with 200 °C. However, too high temperature possibly causes decreasing QY of CDs owing to over-carbonization or aggregation of nanoparticles resulting in the elimination of surrounding fluorophores or passivated surface of core carbon. This gives the synthesized CDs with reduced fluorescence ability when excited (Peng et al., 2021).

The mass yield percentage of CDs synthesized from lignin in ChLa and ChBu, and sucrose using ChUr and ChBu was demonstrated in Fig. 2. The trends of increasing temperature and time resulted in enhanced mass yield percentage of CDs produced from sucrose. However, the mass yield of lignin-derived CDs decreased as the temperature and time of synthesis increased. This contradicts a previous study (Huang et al., 2022) as they found that with increasing synthesis temperature and time, the yield percentage of CDs by mass increased. The decrease in the mass yield of CDs in the present study may be caused by the transformation of small to larger CDs for ChLaLig and ChBuLig that yielded lower mass after cut-off membrane purification (MWCO 1000 Da) that allowed only nanoparticles with the size < 10 nm to get passed. Another reason was possibly due to the excess heat energy delivered to the system via inefficient heat conduction that caused decomposition of the lignin substance to the gaseous products such as CH4, C2H6, CO, and CO2 which are incondensable (Huang et al., 2022; Nxele and Nyokong, 2022). This resulted in a decrease in the CDs content and mass yield.

Fig. 3 illustrates that all CDs produced from lignin using ChLa and ChBu, as well as CDs derived from sucrose in ChBu solvent, exhibited a negative charge. Conversely, CDs synthesized from sucrose using ChUr solvent exhibited an increase in positive charge. The positive surface charge of the ChUrSuc CDs was ascribed to amine groups present on their surface, derived from the ChCl/urea solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence, UV-Vis spectroscopic properties and TEM images of hydrothermal CDs synthesized from lignin and sucrose
ce:section.ce:para: From the results, lignin-derived CDs showed the highest FL emission peak ranging between λEm = 370 and 420 nm ( Fig. 4) while the sucrose-derived CDs showed the greatest emission peak ranging between λEm = 420 and 500 nm ( Fig. 5). The red-shift phenomenon, referred to as the excitation shifted to a longer wavelength, was observed particularly when the precursor was sucrose. The influence of an increased synthesis temperature on red-shift characteristics of CDs was greater than an increased synthesis time.

The UV-Vis absorbance spectra of CDs synthesized from lignin and sucrose at different temperatures and times are shown in Fig. 6. The optical bandgap energy of CDs calculated using Tauc Plots is shown in the inset. The CDs synthesized from lignin which were ChLaLig (Fig. 6A) and ChBuLig (Fig. 6B) showed that the bandgap energy increased in the same range from 3.89 eV to 4.70 eV when the synthesis temperature increased from 200 °C to 240 °C for 6 h. For the sucrose-derived CDs, ChUrSuc (Fig. 6C) and ChBuSuc (Fig. 6D), the bandgap energy likewise increased from 3.02 eV and 3.07–4.71 eV and 4.12 eV, respectively when the temperature enhanced from 200 °C to 240 °C for 6 h. With increasing synthesis temperature, the size of the CDs decreased resulting in increased bandgap energy according to the quantum confinement effect. Increasing the synthesis time has a likely tendency to temperature as an increased synthesis time resulted in a smaller size of the CDs, however it was reported that at high temperatures, CDs from EFB and polyethylene glycol could grow larger with longer hydrothermal time (Sangjan et al., 2022). The dehydration and carbonization conditions significantly affect the sizes and degree of graphitization of CDs, leading to variations in their photoluminescence properties (Xia et al., 2019).

TEM images of CDs from lignin and sucrose synthesized at 220 °C for 10 h were shown in Fig. 7A-D. Nevertheless, the average sizes of CDs are seemingly larger than 10 nm possibly due to aggregation although the surface zeta potentials were high. The smallest size was obtained from ChBuLig220C10h (21.08 ± 4.89 nm) ( Table 2). Following nucleation, small molecules in the solution attach to the nucleus's surface. The nucleus then undergoes further dehydration and carbonization, forming a highly crosslinked structure or carbon skeleton with a graphite-like or diamond-like structure (Ge et al., 2015). This process leads to the development of a new surface and the expansion of the carbon core, resulting in the growth of carbon-based dots. Thus, a longer reaction time produces larger CDs. It was reported that the micronucleus can aggregate due to hydrophilic and hydrophobic effects, similar to micelle formation. In this process, the hydrophobic parts gather inside while the hydrophilic parts surround them on the outside.

From the aforementioned data, we can conclude that the optimal hydrothermal condition was 220 °C for 10 h since it yielded the highest %QY and mass yield. The ChBu solvent demonstrated a substantial mass yield relative to the precursor weight across all experimental conditions. Hence, ChBu was chosen as a solvent for both lignin and sucrose precursors in the synthesis of magnetic hyperthermia CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs synthesis in DESs using magnetic hyperthermia (MHT) method
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of CDs from lignin and sucrose in ChBu solvent was studied using the MHT technique. The effect of different concentrations of iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe3O4-NPs) as 5, 15, and 25 %w/w for synthesis time from 0 s to 180 s on CD yield and characteristics was investigated. In the MHT synthesis of CDs, the process involves placing the substance in a wired vessel of an induction heater, and an alternating current is applied. Fe3O4-NPs were added to induce heat generation due to a strong magnetic field at the molecular level to the carbon precursor through the DES solvent. As shown in Fig. 8A, Fe3O4-NPs concentrations at 5, 15, and 25 %w/w based on lignin precursor had generated different levels of temperature with different ramp rates. At different amounts of 5, 15, and 25 %w/w Fe3O4-NPs, the temperature of the system was increased up to 121.6, 207.5, and 220 °C, respectively within 180 s. The thermal imaging from an infrared camera during MHT synthesis of CD from lignin in ChBu solvent containing 25 %w/w Fe3O4-NPs from 30 s to 180 s was illustrated in Fig. 8B showing the heat generated and reached a constant at approximately 220 °C within 180 s. A good correlation between Fe3O4-NPs content and an increased temperature in the system was observed, and the temperature tended to remain constant over the time when the designed system was operated at atmospheric pressure (Zhu et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum and mass yield of MHT CDs synthesized from lignin and sucrose
ce:section.ce:para: Table 2 demonstrates the properties such as size, %QY, and mass yield of CDs synthesized by the conventional hydrothermal and MHT methods at different times, precursors, and solvents. The CDs synthesized from sucrose using ChBu solvent (ChBuSuc) at 15 %w/w Fe3O4-NPs for 120 s had the highest %QY of 51.52 %. CDs from lignin (ChBuLig) had substantially lower QYs (10.32–23.44 %QY) but markedly higher CDs mass yield (10.87–28.85 wt%) compared to ChBuSuc which produced CDs mass yield as only 0.16–0.18 wt%. CDs synthesized from lignin using ChBu solvent, 25 wt%, Fe3O4-NPs concentration, and 180 s synthesis time (ChBuLig25wt180s) possessed the highest %QY as 23.44 % and mass yield as 28.85 % both, and smallest CD’s size at only 1.83 ± 0.21 nm (Fig. S13, and Table 2).

The results showed that the QY and mass yield of ChBuLig increased with the increasing Fe3O4-NPs loadings as a consequence of higher system temperatures during MHT CDs synthesis. The effect of synthesis time on the change of QY and mass yield percentage of ChBuLig under MHT condition demonstrated that the increase in the synthesis time resulted in an increase in the CD mass yield from 10.87 % to 18.82 %, as well as an enhanced QY from 10.32 % to 15.04 % from ChBuLig15wt120s and ChBuLig15wt180s, respectively. The QY was in good accordance with the XRD peak representing graphitic carbon structure and the crystallinity of carbon at 2-theta 20–22° in XRD chromatograms (Fig. 8C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD, FTIR, and Raman characterizations of MHT CDs from lignin and sucrose
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD diffraction patterns exhibited the carbon nanostructure and crystallinity at 2-theta of 20–22° (Fig. 8C). The highest intensity of carbon crystallinity was obtained from ChBuLig25wt180s > ChBuLig15wt180s > ChBuLig15wt120s > ChBuSuc5wt180s > ChBuSuc15wt120s. Hence, lignin-derived CDs outstandingly exhibited higher XRD crystalline carbon structure at 2θ = 20–22° relative to sucrose-derived CDs. The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 8D revealed various peaks corresponding to functional groups on the surface of CDs, including the stretching vibrations of C–OH at 3300 cm−1 and C–H at 2920 cm−1. The absorption bands for CO and C–O–C were observed at 1750 cm−1 and 1250 cm−1, respectively (Alarfaj et al., 2018). The peak at 1020 cm−1 signifies the C–O stretching bond in aromatic rings (Qiang et al., 2019). It is worth mentioning that the presence of –OH groups with their reducing activity on the surface of CDs especially ChBuLig15wt180s and ChBuLig25wt180s confirmed the ability of CDs to donate electrons during the MHT synthesis, and thus the formation of the CDs/Fe3O4 nanocomposite probably occurred (Gu et al., 2022) which could be confirmed by Raman spectroscopy and XPS analysis.

The Raman spectrum of carbon materials typically features three main bands: D, G, and 2D as illustrated in Fig. S13. The D band, found around 1350 cm⁻¹ , indicates structural defects such as vacancies or distortions within the graphene layers. In contrast, the G band, located near 1570 cm⁻¹ , is associated with the in-plane vibrations of sp² hybridized carbon atoms. The defect density in the material can be assessed by the intensity ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) (Muzyka et al., 2018). As shown in Figure S13(A-E), all MHT-derived CDs show the D band at 1350–1400 cm⁻¹ and the G band at 1570–1580 cm⁻¹ , consistent with values reported in the literature. MHT-derived CDs demonstrated relatively higher ID/IG ratios than those from hydrothermal synthesis at 220 °C for 10 h (Table 1).

Lignin-derived CDs (Figure S13(C-E) gave substantially lower ID/IG ratios (ChBuLig15wt120s with ID/IG = 1.70) than sucrose-derived CDs at the same synthesis condition (ChBuSuc15wt120s with ID/IG = 2.27). The observed lower ID/IG ratios (Table S2) in lignin-derived CDs from the MHT method suggests a substantial presence of higher graphitic carbon and fewer defects. In contrast, sucrose-derived CDs from MHT with higher ID/IG ratio were likely caused by either the presence of defects or the functionalization of graphitic carbon. An increased MHT synthesis time and temperature by increasing %wt Fe3O4-NPs in lignin-derived CDs obviously displayed a decline of ID/IG ratios from 1.70, 1.42, and 1.10 for ChBuLig15 %wt120s, ChBuLig15 %wt180s, and ChBuLig25 %wt180s, respectively. Therefore, among all MHT synthesized CDs, ChBuLig25wt180s was the most outstanding CD with the highest graphitic structure, smallest size (1.83 ± 0.21 nm, Fig. S14), highest mass yield of 28.85 %, and promising QY of 23.44 %.

Fe3O4@CDs nanocomposite structure can be confirmed by the Raman shift as illustrated in Fig. 8E and F. The results exhibited that sucrose-derived CDs namely ChBuSuc5wt180s and ChBuSuc15wt120s contained a trace amount of unreacted Fe3O4 observed at the peak at 668 cm⁻¹ which is attributed to Fe₃O₄ (Majumder et al., 2015), while there is no such peak found in lignin-derived CDs, especially at 180 s of MHT synthesis namely ChBuLig15wt180s, and ChBuLig25wt180s. In addition, no peaks at 299 cm⁻¹ and 412 cm⁻¹ corresponding to α-Fe₂O₃ (Varghese et al., 2020) due to the oxidation of Fe₃O₄ were detected at this condition. However, the C-Fe bonds that were postulated to have occurred in ChBuLig15wt180s, and ChBuLig25wt180s could be accurately confirmed by XPS and TGA analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS and TGA characterization of MHT CDs from lignin and sucrose
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 9, XPS spectra of MHT CDs demonstrated the C1s, O1s, and N1s representing typically chemical bonds in CDs. The Fe3O4@CDs conjugation exhibited four distinct peaks: (i) oxygen in Fe–O bonds within Fe3O4 (529.1 eV), (ii) oxygen in Fe–O–C bonds, likely originating from bidentate coordination between surface Fe and hydroxyl/carbonyl groups (529.7 eV), (iii) oxygen in carbonyl groups (CO, 530.7 eV), and (iv) oxygen in hydroxyl groups (C–O–C/C–O, 532.0 eV) (Hua et al., 2017). For ChBuSuc15wt120 s (Fig. 9A), the C–O–Fe peak (38 %) at binding energy 513.8 eV and Fe–O (3.1 %) at 535.1 eV were observed (Fig. 9A2). In case of MHT CDs from lignin, sulfur peak (S2p) was observed. ChBuLig15wt120s and ChBuLig15wt180s exhibited lower peaks of C–O–Fe and no peak of Fe–O was detected. In contrast, ChBuLig25wt180s showed markedly C–O–Fe peaks (35.8 %) and Fe–O peak (2.1 %) (Fig. 9D2). This was supported by the notable Fe 2p peaks of ChBuLig15wt180s and ChBuLig25wt180s from deconvoluted XPS analysis (Fig. S15). Noticeably, the peaks at 725.0 eV and 711.4 eV correspond to the characteristic signals of Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2, respectively. Additionally, the two broad peaks at 734.2 eV and 718.6 eV are identified as satellite peaks of Fe (Yang et al., 2014). The Fe 2p3/2 peak can be deconvoluted into three distinct peaks at binding energies of 712.9, 711.3, and 710.3 eV, respectively, indicating the presence of Fe–O bonds (Li et al., 2019). All the results of deconvoluted peaks were moreover in good agreement with atomic and mass concentration of CDs in Table S3. The substantially high carbon atomic content was obtained in all lignin derived CDs from MHT method ChBuLig15wt120s (75.2 %), ChBuLig15wt180s (72.8 %), and ChBuLig25wt180s (70.5 %) (Table S3).

The TGA curves and its derivatives (DTG) (Fig. 9E and F, respectively) reveal multiple stages of weight loss occurring at different temperature ranges. The initial weight loss at 80–100 °C corresponds to water evaporation, and the weight loss below 150 °C is likely due to desorption, primarily involving species adsorbed on the surface of the CDs. A second significant weight loss occurs around 327 °C (Sangjan et al., 2022), attributed to the thermal degradation of carbon majority in the CDs. Notably, ChBuLig25wt180s exhibited a pronounced degradation peak at 416 °C, whereas ChBuLig15wt180s displayed a smaller peak at a similar temperature, suggesting the degradation of carbon associated with iron (C-O-Fe). Previous studies reported that weight loss between 300 °C and 540 °C was caused by the transformation of carbon, either free or combined with iron (de Resende et al., 2009). The results agreed with Raman and XPS data analysis. The DTG curves indicate that this process unfolds in two distinct steps (∼327 °C and ∼416 °C), potentially reflecting the sequential thermal degradation of various forms of carbon.

The high purity of MHT CDs could be further confirmed by TGA. The results demonstrated that ChBuLig25wt180s has the highest purity represented by the lowest solid residue at 13.21 % after heating from 25 °C to 900 °C under N2 atmosphere (Table S2 and Fig. 9E and F). In addition, the XPS analysis results (Table S3) showed the high purity of carbon in terms of atomic concentration (71 %) and mass concentration (61 %) of carbon in ChBuLig25wt180s. Moreover, Raman spectroscopy of ChBuLig25wt180s gave the lowest ID/IG ratio of 1.10 (Table S2) displaying the strongest intensity of graphitic carbon (G band) at 1570 cm−1 over the defects or disordered carbon (D band) at 1390 cm−1 (Fig. S13E). Therefore, the MHT derived CDs from lignin in ChBu at sufficient molecular heating (> 200 °C) and time (≥ 3 min) facilitate an effective CDs production with high yield and promising characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and UV-Vis spectroscopic properties of MHT CDs from lignin and sucrose
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence characterization of CDs from ChBuLig and ChBuSuc is illustrated in Fig. 10A-E. The FL emission intensity and wavelength at varying excitation wavelengths from 240 to 400 nm were recorded. CDs derived from ChBuSuc showed FL emission peak at the wavelength ranging from λEm = 400–550 nm when the excitation wavelength was between λEx = 280 and 360 nm as depicted in Fig. 10A at 5 %w/w Fe3O4-NPs, and Fig. 10B at 15 %w/w Fe3O4-NPs. This can be explained as a red-shift phenomenon in which the absorption and emission spectra shift towards a longer wavelength. An increase in Fe3O4-NPs concentration substantially enhanced FL emission intensity since the greater synthesis temperature led to enhanced graphitic carbon structure of CDs, and thus enhanced %QY which was almost two times. In contrast, ChBuLig exhibited excitation wavelength-dependent characteristics in which the maximum emission in the range of λEm = 350–400 nm was achieved only when the excitation wavelength was λEx = 280–300 nm under UV irradiation as demonstrated in Fig. 10C–E. An enhanced Fe3O4-NPs concentration (25 %wt) and synthesis time (180 s) substantially increased the FL intensity of CDs for the ChBuLig system (Fig. 10E).

The photonic bandgap energy of CDs synthesized by the MHT method is shown in Fig. 10F and G. A substantially narrower band gap of CDs from ChBuSuc was found in the MHT method (Fig. 10F) with an energy band gap of 2.85 eV which was much lower than that for the ChBuLig (Fig. 10G). This finding indicates that the CDs synthesized from sucrose via MHT have either a larger size or denser surface passivated molecules so-called fluorophores compared with CDs from lignin. ChBuLig showed similar bandgap energy in a range of 3.90–3.97 eV with an average value of 3.95 ± 0.04 eV for every condition (Fig. 10G). Since the ChBuLig was combined with Fe3O4-NPs during MHT, it has been noted that the number of free electrons and/or defects with low energy from incoming photons can greatly affect the transmission spectrum of the dielectric material (Abdulkareem et al., 2022). The sample with a smaller crystalline size shows a larger energy gap because of the quantum confinement phenomenon (Klimov, 2007). These results are in line with earlier studies on Fe3O4-NPs (Kouotou et al., 2018). These bandgap values were even greater than those obtained from ChBuLig from a conventional batch hydrothermal method at 200 and 220 °C for 6 h and 10 h from the previous experiments showing that much smaller size of CDs from lignin was achieved from MHT method which could be confirmed by FETEM imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FETEM characterization of hydrothermal and MHT CDs from lignin and sucrose
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 7E–H and Fig. S14, the FETEM imaging results revealed that all CDs from the MHT method were smaller than those from the conventional hydrothermal method, therefore the findings displayed higher band gap energy values caused by smaller particle size. The average size of CDs in the hydrothermal synthesis system was 158.36 ± 31.84 nm, 21.08 ± 4.89 nm, 68.3 ± 24.04 nm, and 136.69 ± 49.22 nm for ChLaLig220C10h, ChBuLig220C10h, ChUrSuc220C10h, and ChBuSuc220C10h, respectively (Table 2). The large size of CDs over 20 nm could potentially be attributed to their aggregation in the medium, resulting in an increase in their overall size. In contrast, the size of all the CDs synthesized from the MHT method was lower than 15 nm. CD size was considerably smaller when synthesized via the MHT approach using Fe3O4-NPs as the magnetic-induced heating medium. The measured CDs sizes were 5.21 ± 1.24 nm (ChBuLig15wt120s), 10.99 ± 4.11 nm (ChBuLig15wt180s), 1.83 ± 0.21 nm (ChBuLig25wt180s), 15.57 ± 5.82 nm (ChBuSuc5wt180s), and 10.27 ± 5.99 nm (ChBuSuc15wt120s) (Table 2). The smallest size of CDs 1.83 ± 0.21 nm was obtained from ChBuLig25wt180s in which CDs were produced from lignin in ChBu solvent with a 25 %wt Fe3O4-NPs concentration for 180 s (Fig. S14). This ChBuLig25wt180s condition in addition achieved the highest %QY as 23.44 % and mass highest yield as 28.85 % (Table 2). The smaller size of CDs from the MHT method compared with the conventional hydrothermal method might be attributed to its rapid, specific, controlled, and homogeneous heating, which facilitates quick nucleation and inhibits overgrowth and aggregation of the CDs being formed. Therefore, the MHT approach seems to be important in facilitating the production of small-sized CDs with a high level of efficiency and the ability to accurately control the particle size (Jiananda et al., 2023).

A comparison of CDs obtained from hydrothermal and MHT synthesis was done by selecting the optimum conditions regarding the highest QY and mass yield of hydrothermal CDs synthesis for ChBuLig at 220 °C for 10 h, compared to the MHT synthesis from ChBuLig for 3 min (180 s) time. The operating cost based on exclusively electrical power usage and synthesis duration for each batch of CD synthesis is summarized in Table 3. The results demonstrated that the synthesis of CDs by the MHT method can substantially reduce the electricity cost of CD synthesis from 1.220 USD down to 0.091 USD per batch (∼13.41 times). MHT could increase the CD mass yield from 5.61 mg CDs to 62.72 mg CDs/g substrate/h. Therefore, CD production capacity from MHT synthesis was 1018 % or 10.18 times greater than the conventional hydrothermal method which requires an exogenous heat source from an electrical heater. Consequently, the synthesis of CDs by MHT is, therefore, an alternative and promising option. The advantage over the hydrothermal method is a significantly faster synthesis time that can reduce production costs and a higher CD yield compared to the conventional hydrothermal method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The peroxidase-like activity of CDs for H2O2 detection
ce:section.ce:para: The enhancement and limitation of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) detection are of paramount importance and have garnered wide interest due to its importance as a central molecule in biological systems in very small quantities. It has been confirmed that colorimetry of nanozymes having light-emitting properties enhances test ability, and sensitivity, and reduces the detection limit of nanozyme surface interference properties. In this study, a successful H2O2 detection test has been developed using the colorimetric analysis of the oxidative reaction catalyzed by magnetic hyperthermia CDs-derived nanozymes. The enhanced colorimetric assay of TMB oxidation that represents H2O2 cleavage is expected to depend on the growth or increase in the size of the magnetic hyperthermia CDs-derived nanozymes reaction accelerator. The CDs from MHT could potentially contain Fe from magnetic hyperthermia, as well as oxygen-containing functional groups of C−OH, C−OOH, and C−OR, when R is CH3 ascribed from ChBu on the surface of CDs. Previous tests using UV-Vis spectroscopy revealed that CDs/PtNPs demonstrated better synergy in hydrogen peroxide reduction compared to CDs and PtNPs alone (Saengsrichan et al., 2022a). The present work was achieved by using magnetic inducive CDs synthesized in ChBu from lignin and sucrose, which were nanozyme to catalyze the H2O2 dissociation to OH radicals. Subsequently, TMB oxidation was monitored by UV-Vis absorbance at 652 nm as shown in Fig. 11.

To study the sensitivity of CDs from ChBuLig and ChBuSuc at different percent loadings of Fe3O4 during magnetic induction synthesis of CDs for detecting the color change of TMB upon oxidation, UV-Vis spectroscopy at 562 nm was conducted for 0–30 min at room temperature (25 °C) at the concentrations of 1 mM H2O2 and 5 mM TMB, as shown in Fig. 11A. For the pseudo-first-order kinetic of peroxidase-like activity study, as shown in Fig. 11B, the higher reaction rate or greater rate constant (k) of reaction during 0–9 min was obtained from CDs derived from ChBuLig 25 %wt Fe3O4 for 180 s at the velocity of 0.28 a.u. min−1, subsequent nanozymes that provided high kinetic rate were ChBuSuc at 15 wt% Fe3O4 for 120 s (0.092 a.u. min−1) > ChBuLig at 15 wt% Fe3O4 for 180 s (0.0875 a.u. min−1) > ChBuLig at 15 wt% Fe3O4 for 120 s (0.0598 a.u. min−1) > ChBuSuc at 5 wt% Fe3O4 for 180 s (0.0116 a.u. min−1) > the control (No CDs) (0.0073 a.u. min−1). The intensity of color representing the amount of oxidized TMB is illustrated in Fig. 11C.

Based on the results, CDs synthesized from ChBuLig 25 %wt Fe3O4 for 180 s (ChBuLig25wt180s) instantly increased the oxidation of TMB within only 6 min, and after that, the intensity of solution color was relatively constant. This was possibly due to high rate of H2O2 decomposition which may deplete all available H2O2 before the TMB oxidation reaction completes. In other words, the dissolution rate of H2O2 to hydroxide ions (OH−) and hydroxyl radicals ( ⋅ OH) occurs faster than the rate of TMB oxidation. Particularly, too severe a H2O2 decomposition rate overcomes the mass transfer rate of hydroxide ions (OH−) and hydroxyl radicals ( ⋅ OH) to transfer to TMB molecules. Thus, the effect of Fe loading on CDs, which greatly influences the ramp rate of temperature and the final temperature of the reaction, plays a greater role than the MHT duration for CD synthesis resulting in significantly enhanced CD’s catalytic peroxidase mimetic ability as a nanozyme.

When comparing surface-modified CDs from the MHT method, there is a reduction in the oxygen content of the −OH functional group on the surface of CDs, particularly ChBuLig25wt180s and ChBuLig15wt180s, which was indicated by FTIR spectra (Fig. 8D). This agreed with the XPS results of Fe−C and Fe-O-C bonds of both aforementioned MHT synthesized CDs. However, in the presence of Fe3O4 during CDs synthesis using the MHT method, the ketonic carbonyl group (−CO), as well as C−O and O−C−O groups were substantially enhanced and thus facilitated the catalytic activity of H2O2 decomposition of ChBuLig compared to ChBuSuc. When compared to hydroxyl groups, ketonic carbonyl groups have higher catalytic activity towards H2O2 reduction. However, the ChBuLig25wt180s and ChBuLig15wt180s which possibly contain heteroatoms, such as C-O-Fe from Fe3O4-NPs, can enhance the H2O2 reduction reaction at room temperature with the supreme kinetic rate. The primary reason is the complexation with a substrate, namely H2O2, which becomes more efficient in terms of affinity toward these two nanozymes possessing dense (−CO), C−O, and O−C−O groups, and thus provides higher electron transfer rates. Therefore, CDs derived from ChBuLig at 25 %wt Fe3O4 (180 s) substantially increased electron transfer efficiency and accelerated the decomposition rate of H2O2 to OH¯ and ⋅ OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were successfully synthesized from lignin, EFB, glucose, sucrose, and xylose employing DESs by a hydrothermal technique. The resulting CDs were found to be in nano-size. The conditions that yielded an acceptable percentage of quantum yield and mass yield has been identified at a temperature of 220 °C for 10 h. CDs were subsequently synthesized from lignin and sucrose utilizing ChBu as a DES using the magnetic hyperthermia technique. The effect of Fe3O4 concentration can cause the temperature to rise rapidly in 3 min and generate the CDs products with fluorescence properties, confirming the characteristics of CDs based on structural properties. The magnetic hyperthermia synthesis time increased from 120 to 180 s and temperature from 200 to 220 °C with different magnetic nanoparticle loadings resulting in an increased quantum yield and mass yield. Magnetic hyperthermia intensively reduced the production cost by 13.41 times lower than conventional hydrothermal reaction due to the shorter time, and faster heat transfer rate toward the site of carbon nanomolecular structure with low cost, and higher productivity in an easy-to-perform way.

Enhanced peroxidase mimetic activity or H2O2 decomposition rate to generate hydroxide ions (OH¯) and hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) was achieved by CDs derived from ChBuLig at 25 %wt Fe3O4 NPs (180 s) observed by TMB oxidation. The effect of Fe3O4-NPs loading during MHT synthesis which greatly influences the ramp rate of temperature and the final temperature of the reaction plays a greater role than the effect of the duration of CDs synthesis in this magnetic inductive technique on the catalytic activity for peroxidase mimetic ability of the nanozyme. Through a critical analysis of recent advancements and innovative methodologies, we elucidate the potential of ultrafast magnetic-assisted DES synthesis as a transformative paradigm for CD fabrication. By emphasizing key mechanistic insights and highlighting emerging trends, we aim to provide researchers with invaluable aspects for harnessing this innovative approach to unlock new frontiers in nanomaterials science and technology.
